Minds that made money · 02
He beat the market for 60 years.
Mostly by sitting still.
19.8% a year — while almost everyone else churned.
Meet the world's most patient investor →
02
Warren
Buffett
Born 1930 · runs Berkshire Hathaway
Took over a failing textile mill in 1965.
Turned it into one of the world's biggest companies — by buying slowly and almost never selling.
19.8% a year vs the S&P's 10.2% — for ~60 years
Berkshire Hathaway compounded at 19.8%/yr (1965–2023) vs the S&P 500's 10.2%. Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2023 shareholder letter.
03
$1,000 in 1965.
Two very different lives.
With Buffett
$44M
19.8% a year
In the S&P 500
$313K
10.2% a year
Same $1,000. Same 60 years. ~140× the money — for waiting.
$1,000 invested 1965→2023: Berkshire +4,384,748% ≈ $43.8M; S&P 500 +31,223% ≈ $313K. Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2023 shareholder letter. Illustrative, rounded.
04
One position, since 1988
He bought Coca-Cola once.
Never sold a share.
The dividend alone now hands back his entire cost every ~1.6 years. Forever.
Berkshire built a ~$1.3B Coca-Cola stake (1988–94, ~400M shares) and has never sold one. Sources: Berkshire filings; Coca-Cola dividend disclosures.

Lethargy bordering on sloth remains the cornerstone of our investment style.

What most people miss →
06
Everyone quotes "be greedy when others are fearful."
The harder rule is boring:
most days, the right move is no move.
He reads 5–6 hours a day — mostly to decide to do nothing.
07
So what do you actually take from him?
Investing isn't about good decisions.
It's not making bad ones
when you're bored.
Your portfolio doesn't need more of your attention. It needs less.
08
One question
How long do you take to decide to buy?
How long to sell?

09
He didn't out-trade the market.
He out-waited it.
Now you've got

Education only. Not investment advice. Figures are rounded and illustrative. Sources: Berkshire Hathaway 2023 shareholder letter; Berkshire / Coca-Cola filings.

Caption — Instagram post body

Warren Buffett beat the market for 60 years — mostly by sitting still. From 1965 to 2023, Berkshire Hathaway compounded at 19.8% a year while the S&P 500 did 10.2%. That gap sounds small. It isn't.

$1,000 invested with Buffett in 1965 became roughly $44 million. The same $1,000 in the S&P 500: about $313,000. Same start, same six decades — about 140 times the money, for waiting.

His clearest example: he bought Coca-Cola in 1988, built a ~$1.3 billion stake, and has never sold a single share. The dividend alone now hands Berkshire back its entire original cost every year and a half or so. As he wrote in 1990: "Lethargy bordering on sloth remains the cornerstone of our investment style."

Everyone quotes "be greedy when others are fearful." The harder rule is the boring one: most days, the right move is no move. He reads five to six hours a day — mostly to decide to do nothing. Investing isn't really about making good decisions; it's about not making bad ones when you're bored. Your portfolio doesn't need more of your attention. It needs less.

So: how long do you take to decide to buy? How long to sell? And why is one so much faster than the other?

Education only, not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: Berkshire Hathaway 2023 shareholder letter; Berkshire / Coca-Cola filings.

Save this for your next itchy trigger finger — and send it to someone who checks their portfolio five times a day.
